title: Figurative Language in Scholarly and Literary Discourse
learningObjectives: Analyze sophisticated phrasal verbs in scholarly writing
Understand literary and philosophical idioms at near-native level
Evaluate the rhetorical function of figurative language
Deploy phrasal verbs and idioms with native-like precision

sections.title: Mastery of Figurative Language
sections.content: At the C2 level, figurative language is not merely decorative — it is a precision instrument for intellectual discourse. Phrasal verbs and idioms in academic, literary, and philosophical writing carry layers of meaning that require deep cultural and linguistic competence. This reading explores how mastery-level English deploys these tools.

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs in Philosophical Writing
sections.content:
Philosophical discourse employs phrasal verbs with remarkable precision:

The philosopher grappled with the aporia at the heart of Western ontology. She sought to tease out the implications of categorical imperatives while pushing back against reductive epistemology. Her work drew on heteroglossia to open up new avenues of inquiry, ultimately arriving at a position that did away with the vicious circularity of previous arguments.

Philosophical phrasal verbs:
• Grapple with - struggle intellectually with a difficult concept
• Tease out - carefully extract or identify subtle elements
• Push back against - resist or challenge
• Draw on - use as a resource or influence
• Open up - create new possibilities
• Do away with - eliminate or abolish
• Arrive at - reach through reasoning

sections.title: Idioms in Literary Criticism
sections.content:
Literary criticism employs idioms that bridge the scholarly and the evocative:

• Read between the lines - Understand implicit meaning. 'The subaltern narrative requires us to read between the lines of colonial texts.'
• A far cry from - Very different from. 'This palimpsest of meaning is a far cry from surface-level interpretation.'
• Strike a chord - Resonate emotionally. 'The ekphrasis in the poem strikes a chord with readers across cultures.'
• Cut to the chase - Get to the essential point. 'To cut to the chase: the topology of the argument is fundamentally flawed.'
• The jury is still out - No conclusion yet. 'The jury is still out on whether qualia can be empirically studied.'
• A tall order - A very difficult task. 'Reconciling inviolability with jurisprudence is a tall order.'

sections.title: Mastery-Level Usage
sections.content:
Example 1: 'The scholar teased out the heteroglossia embedded in the palimpsest, drawing on postcolonial theory to push back against essentialist readings.'

Example 2: 'The pathophysiology of the condition opened up new avenues for research, though the requisite interdisciplinary approach proved a tall order.'

Example 3: 'By grappling with the aporia of free will, the philosopher arrived at a position that did away with the vicious circularity of compatibilist arguments.'

Example 4: 'The arbitrage between competing theoretical frameworks is a far cry from simple eclecticism — it requires reading between the lines of each tradition.'

sections.title: The Rhetorical Power of Figurative Language
sections.content:
At the C2 level, understanding why a writer chooses a phrasal verb or idiom is as important as understanding what it means:

'Tease out' implies patience and delicacy — the writer is handling fragile ideas.
'Push back against' implies intellectual courage — the writer is challenging authority.
'Grapple with' implies honest struggle — the writer acknowledges difficulty.
'Do away with' implies decisive action — the writer is making a bold claim.

These choices are never accidental. They signal the writer's stance, confidence, and relationship to the material.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• C2 figurative language carries rhetorical and philosophical weight
• Phrasal verbs in scholarly writing signal intellectual stance
• Literary idioms bridge analytical precision and evocative power
• The choice of figurative language reveals the writer's relationship to their subject
• Mastery means not just understanding but deploying these tools strategically

keyVocabulary.definition: To struggle intellectually with a complex problem
keyVocabulary.example: Philosophers grapple with questions of consciousness.

keyVocabulary.definition: To carefully extract or identify subtle elements
keyVocabulary.example: The critic teased out hidden themes in the novel.

keyVocabulary.definition: To resist or challenge an idea or authority
keyVocabulary.example: Scholars push back against reductive interpretations.

keyVocabulary.definition: To use something as a resource or influence
keyVocabulary.example: The theory draws on multiple disciplines.
The new framework does away with outdated assumptions.

keyVocabulary.definition: To understand the implicit or hidden meaning
keyVocabulary.example: You need to read between the lines of the report.
This analysis is a far cry from simplistic readings.

keyVocabulary.definition: No definitive conclusion has been reached
keyVocabulary.example: The jury is still out on this theory.

grammarPoints.explanation: At the C2 level, phrasal verbs are chosen not just for meaning but for rhetorical effect. 'Grapple with' implies honest intellectual struggle, while 'deal with' is neutral. 'Tease out' implies delicacy, while 'extract' is clinical.
commonMistakes: Using neutral verbs when a phrasal verb would add rhetorical power to academic writing
